courses.title: Your First Win
courses.description: Skip the theory. Open Claude, write your first prompt, and walk away with something useful in 10 minutes.

lessons.title: Open Claude, Get Results
lessons.description: Your first 5 minutes with Claude. Zero throat-clearing — just results.

sections.content: Let's get you a win. By the end of this lesson, you'll have gotten Claude to produce something useful for you. No backstory, no theory — just results.
Go to claude.ai and type something you actually need help with. An email, a summary, a plan — anything. Here's what a good first prompt looks like vs a bad one.
See the difference? Specific prompts get specific results. Let's practice writing one.
Now let's see how a real conversation with Claude flows. You'll pick the best response at each step.
You just wrote a real prompt, iterated on it, and got a polished result. That's the core Claude workflow: prompt, review, refine.
You just got your first win with Claude. You know how to write a specific prompt and iterate to get what you want. Next up: building something real.

exercises.prompt: Two prompts, two results. One is vague and gets generic output. The other is specific and gets exactly what you need. Pick the better one.
exercises.correct_answer:
{"rounds":[{"prompt":"You asked Claude to help you write an email to your manager about working from home.","response_a":"Subject: WFH Request

Hi Manager,

I would like to work from home on Tuesdays and Thursdays. Working remotely helps me focus on deep work without office distractions. I have been consistently meeting all deadlines and my productivity metrics have stayed strong during previous remote days. I am happy to discuss a trial period if that helps. Let me know your thoughts.

Best,
Alex","response_b":"Subject: Quick Question

Hey,

Would it be okay if I worked from home sometimes? I think I get more done at home honestly. A lot of people on the team already do it so I figured I would ask. Let me know I guess.

Thanks","correct":"a","explanation":"Both are similar length, but Response A is specific (which days, why, evidence of productivity, offers a trial period) while Response B is vague and informal. Specificity and professionalism get better results.","highlights_good":["Tuesdays and Thursdays","meeting all deadlines","productivity metrics","trial period"],"highlights_bad":["sometimes","I think","I guess","A lot of people"]}]}
exercises.explanation: Specificity is the single biggest lever for getting useful output from Claude. Tell it who, what, why, and how — and it delivers.
hints: Look at which response you could actually send without editing.

lessons.title: Your First Real Output
lessons.description: Build a complete, useful document using the Role-Context-Task-Format framework.

sections.content: Time to build something you can actually use. We're going to construct a perfect prompt piece by piece, then watch Claude deliver.
Every great prompt has 4 parts: a Role (who Claude should be), Context (background info), a Task (what you need), and Format (how you want it delivered).
Great structure! But the first draft is never the final draft. Let's practice the iteration loop.
Three rounds of feedback turned a generic letter into a personalized, compelling one. That's the power of iteration.
You just built a professional cover letter from scratch using the Role-Context-Task-Format framework. That same framework works for any output you need from Claude.

exercises.prompt: Build a cover letter prompt using the Role + Context + Task + Format framework. Drag the pieces into the right order.
exercises.correct_answer: {"pieces":["You are an experienced career coach and resume writer","I'm a marketing manager with 5 years of experience applying for a Senior Marketing Director role at a tech startup","Write a cover letter that highlights my leadership experience and data-driven approach","Keep it under 400 words, use a confident but not arrogant tone, and include a strong opening hook"],"correct_order":[0,1,2,3],"context":"Build a cover letter prompt using Role + Context + Task + Format"}
exercises.explanation: Role, Context, Task, Format — in that order. This structure tells Claude exactly who to be, what you need, and how to deliver it.
hints: Start with who Claude should be, then give your background, then the task, then formatting details.

exercises.prompt: Claude wrote a cover letter draft. Now iterate on it — each round should make it measurably better.
exercises.correct_answer: {"setup":"You just sent the prompt you built. Claude wrote a draft cover letter.","turns":[{"context":"Claude produced a decent but generic cover letter. The structure is solid but it reads like a template. What do you say?","options":[{"text":"Make the opening more attention-grabbing — start with a bold claim or a surprising result from my career, not 'I am writing to express my interest.'","correct":true,"response":"Revised opening: 'In my last role, I turned a $50K marketing budget into $2M in pipeline revenue in 18 months. Now I want to do it at a company where speed and creativity matter more than process — which is why your Senior Marketing Director role caught my eye.'"},{"text":"It's fine, send it","correct":false,"explanation":"A generic cover letter gets lost in the pile. One round of specific feedback could be the difference between an interview and the trash folder."},{"text":"Start over completely","correct":false,"explanation":"The structure was good — only the opening needed work. Iterating on what works is always faster than starting from scratch."}]},{"context":"Much stronger opening. But the middle section is still generic. What's your next feedback?","options":[{"text":"Add a specific metric from my experience — mention that I grew our email list from 5K to 80K subscribers and launched 3 product lines. Replace the vague 'leadership experience' paragraph with these specifics.","correct":true,"response":"Updated middle section: 'I grew our subscriber base from 5K to 80K in two years and launched three product lines that each hit $500K in first-year revenue. I don't just manage marketing teams — I build growth engines.'"},{"text":"Make it longer","correct":false,"explanation":"Hiring managers skim cover letters. Adding length without adding substance hurts you. Add specific achievements instead."},{"text":"Change to casual tone","correct":false,"explanation":"A cover letter for a director role should stay professional. The current confident tone is appropriate — focus on strengthening the content."}]},{"context":"Now the letter has a strong opening and specific metrics. One more round.","options":[{"text":"Add a closing that references the company's recent product launch — show I did my research. Then end with a clear call to action for a conversation.","correct":true,"response":"Final closing: 'I noticed you just launched [Product] last month — the positioning is sharp and the market timing is perfect. I'd love to talk about how I can help you scale what's clearly already working. When works for a 20-minute call this week?'"},{"text":"Perfect, done","correct":false,"explanation":"The letter is strong but the closing is still generic. Adding a company-specific reference shows genuine interest and research."}]},{"context":"The final cover letter has a bold opening, specific metrics, and a personalized close. Compare the first draft to the final version.","options":[{"text":"The iterated version is dramatically better — it went from a template to a compelling, personalized letter in 3 rounds of feedback.","correct":true,"response":"Exactly. Three specific pieces of feedback transformed a generic template into a letter that demonstrates results, specifics, and genuine interest. This is what iteration looks like."},{"text":"The first draft was fine, the changes were unnecessary","correct":false,"explanation":"Compare the two side by side. The first draft could be from anyone. The final version could only be from you — that's the power of iteration."},{"text":"I should have written more detailed initial prompt instead","correct":false,"explanation":"You could, but iteration is often faster and more natural. It's easier to react to a draft than to specify everything upfront."}]}]}
exercises.explanation: Four rounds of specific feedback turned a template into a personalized, compelling cover letter. Each round addressed one specific weakness.
hints: Give Claude one specific thing to improve each round — don't try to fix everything at once.

lessons.title: Pick the Right Model
lessons.description: See the real difference between Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku — and learn when to use each one.

sections.content: Not all Claudes are the same. Opus thinks deepest, Sonnet is the everyday workhorse, Haiku is the speed demon. Let's see the difference firsthand.
Opus for deep thinking. Haiku for quick tasks. Sonnet for everything in between. The trick is matching the model to the job.
Let's practice choosing the right model for real scenarios.
You can now pick the right Claude model for any task. Use Opus for depth, Sonnet for everyday work, Haiku for speed and scale.

exercises.prompt: Same task, different models. See how the right model makes all the difference.
exercises.correct_answer: {"rounds":[{"prompt":"You asked Claude to explain a contract indemnification clause.","response_a":"This clause means the vendor covers legal costs if their product causes problems. It is a standard provision in most service contracts. You should make sure the scope is reasonable and check if there is a cap on liability before signing.","response_b":"This indemnification clause creates a conditional liability shield. The vendor assumes financial responsibility for third-party claims arising from their deliverables, but only when caused by their negligence or willful misconduct. Key risk: the phrase \"sole negligence\" narrows the trigger significantly. Recommendation: negotiate to remove \"sole\" so the clause covers contributory negligence scenarios as well.","correct":"b","explanation":"Both explain the clause, but Response B identifies a specific risk (\"sole negligence\" language), explains why it matters, and gives an actionable recommendation. Response A is accurate but surface-level.","highlights_good":["sole negligence","narrows the trigger","negotiate to remove"],"highlights_bad":["standard provision","should make sure"]},{"prompt":"Classify this customer email as complaint, question, or praise: 'The package arrived on time. Thanks.'","response_a":"Classification: Positive/Praise. Customer confirms timely delivery and says thanks.","response_b":"Classification: Positive. Sentiment breakdown: 'arrived on time' = logistics satisfaction (positive). 'Thanks' = polite acknowledgment (positive). Overall confidence: 95%. Sub-classification: low-effort praise. Recommended action: No follow-up needed. NPS likelihood: 7-8 range. Customer segment: satisfied-passive.","correct":"a","explanation":"For simple classification, Response A (Haiku) nails it in one line. Response B over-analyzes a two-sentence email. When the task is simple, the faster and cheaper model wins.","highlights_good":["Classification: Positive/Praise"],"highlights_bad":["Sentiment breakdown","NPS likelihood: 7-8 range"]}]}
exercises.explanation: Opus catches nuance that simpler models miss. Haiku is perfect when the task doesn't need deep analysis. Matching model to task is a real skill.
hints: Ask yourself: does this task require deep reasoning, or just quick classification?

exercises.prompt: Match each Claude model to the task it handles best.
exercises.correct_answer: {"Opus":"Write a legal brief analyzing a complex contract","Sonnet":"Draft a professional email to your team","Haiku":"Sort 10,000 customer tickets into categories"}
exercises.explanation: Opus for deep analysis, Sonnet for everyday work, Haiku for high-volume simple tasks. Each model has its sweet spot.
hints: Think about complexity and volume — deep thinking vs. quick processing.

exercises.prompt: You have three tasks today. Pick the right Claude model for each one.
exercises.correct_answer: {"setup":"You're deciding which Claude model to use for different tasks today.","turns":[{"context":"Task 1: I need to analyze a 50-page research paper and find contradictions in the methodology.","options":[{"text":"Opus — this needs deep reasoning to catch subtle contradictions","correct":true,"response":"Good call. Finding contradictions in academic methodology requires careful, nuanced reading. Opus will catch things that faster models would miss."},{"text":"Sonnet — it's just reading a document","correct":false,"explanation":"Sonnet can read it, but finding subtle contradictions in methodology requires the deepest reasoning. This is exactly what Opus is built for."},{"text":"Haiku — it's the fastest","correct":false,"explanation":"Speed doesn't help when you need to catch nuanced logical contradictions in dense academic writing. Haiku would miss the subtle issues."}]},{"context":"Task 2: I need to translate 200 product descriptions from English to Spanish.","options":[{"text":"Haiku — bulk task, speed and cost matter most","correct":true,"response":"Perfect. Translation of short product descriptions is a well-defined task. Haiku handles it quickly at a fraction of the cost, and the quality is excellent for straightforward translation."},{"text":"Opus — I want the best translation quality","correct":false,"explanation":"Opus would give marginally better literary translation, but for 200 product descriptions, Haiku's quality is excellent and you'll save 90% on cost and time."},{"text":"Sonnet — play it safe","correct":false,"explanation":"Sonnet works but you're overpaying for a bulk task. When translating hundreds of similar items, Haiku's speed and cost advantage wins."}]},{"context":"Task 3: I want to brainstorm marketing slogans for a new product launch.","options":[{"text":"Sonnet — creative but not complex, balanced quality and speed","correct":true,"response":"Exactly right. Brainstorming slogans needs creativity but not PhD-level reasoning. Sonnet gives you great creative output at a reasonable cost."},{"text":"Opus — creativity needs the best model","correct":false,"explanation":"Opus is great at creativity, but slogan brainstorming doesn't require deep analysis. Sonnet is plenty creative for this task and much faster."},{"text":"Haiku — just give me quick ideas","correct":false,"explanation":"Haiku can generate ideas but they'll be more generic. Sonnet's extra creativity is worth it for marketing copy that needs to be compelling."}]}]}
exercises.explanation: Model selection is about matching capability to complexity. Deep analysis = Opus. Everyday creative work = Sonnet. Bulk simple tasks = Haiku.
hints: Consider what the task actually requires — deep reasoning, balanced quality, or raw speed.

exercises.prompt: The default model on claude.ai is Claude ___.
exercises.correct_answer: Sonnet
exercises.explanation: Sonnet is the default because it offers the best balance of capability, speed, and cost for everyday tasks.
hints: It's the 'balanced' model — not the most powerful, not the fastest, but the best all-rounder.

exercises.prompt: True or false — test your model knowledge in rapid fire!
exercises.correct_answer: [{"question":"Opus is the cheapest model","answer":false},{"question":"Haiku is best for bulk classification","answer":true},{"question":"Sonnet is the default on claude.ai","answer":true},{"question":"You should always use Opus for the best results","answer":false},{"question":"Haiku can analyze complex legal documents as well as Opus","answer":false},{"question":"Sonnet balances speed, cost, and capability","answer":true},{"question":"You can test the same prompt across different models","answer":true},{"question":"Model choice doesn't affect output quality","answer":false}]
exercises.explanation: Model choice matters. Using the right model for the right task saves money without sacrificing quality.
hints: Think about the tradeoffs between power, speed, and cost.

lessons.title: Make It Better — The Iteration Loop
lessons.description: Master the generate-review-refine cycle that separates beginners from power users.

sections.content: The difference between good and great Claude output is iteration. Beginners accept the first draft. Power users refine it. Let's make you a power user.
The iteration loop: Generate, Review, Give specific feedback, Generate again. Most people skip the 'specific feedback' step. That's the unlock.
Five turns. Each one made it measurably better. That's the loop in action.
Notice how reordering the same pieces changes everything. Structure matters as much as content.
You now iterate like a power user. Generate, review, give specific feedback, repeat. This single skill will 10x your Claude output quality.

exercises.prompt: Write a product launch announcement through 5 rounds of iteration. Each round should make it measurably better.
exercises.correct_answer: {"setup":"You're writing a product launch announcement for your company's new AI feature.","turns":[{"context":"You send a basic prompt: 'Write a product launch announcement for our new AI scheduling feature.' Claude gives you a generic corporate announcement. What's your feedback?","options":[{"text":"Make it more exciting and less corporate — this reads like a press release from 2005, not a product launch people should be thrilled about","correct":true,"response":"Updated: 'We just shipped something big. Our new AI scheduling feature handles it in seconds...'"},{"text":"It's fine","correct":false,"explanation":"'Fine' doesn't get you to great. The tone was too corporate for a product launch. One specific note could transform it."},{"text":"Rewrite it completely differently","correct":false,"explanation":"Too vague — differently how? Give Claude a specific direction: more exciting, less corporate, different angle."}]},{"context":"Better energy, but still generic. No one knows WHY they should care. What's next?","options":[{"text":"Add a specific customer pain point this solves — mention that our users were spending an average of 4.5 hours per week on scheduling before this feature","correct":true,"response":"Updated: 'Our users were spending 4.5 hours every week just scheduling meetings. That's a full month of your life every year lost to calendar Tetris. Our new AI feature cuts that to zero...'"},{"text":"Make it longer","correct":false,"explanation":"Longer doesn't mean better. What it needs is a specific reason to care — a pain point with real numbers."},{"text":"Add more buzzwords","correct":false,"explanation":"Buzzwords are exactly the problem. Specific customer pain points are far more compelling than 'leveraging synergies.'"}]},{"context":"Now it has real urgency. But the opening could be even stronger. What do you say?","options":[{"text":"The opening line should start with a surprising stat or question — something that makes people stop scrolling. Try: 'You lost 234 hours to scheduling last year. A strong hook is the difference between people reading and people scrolling past."}]},{"context":"Strong hook, clear pain point. One more thing missing — there's no reason to act NOW.","options":[{"text":"Add a clear call-to-action at the end — something like 'Try it free for 14 days. No credit card. Tell people exactly what to do next."},{"text":"Add a FAQ section","correct":false,"explanation":"A FAQ dilutes the momentum. Save that for the landing page. The announcement should drive one action: try it."}]},{"context":"Five iterations. Compare the first draft to the final version.","options":[{"text":"Every round fixed one specific thing: tone, pain point, hook, and CTA. The final version is dramatically better than the first draft.","correct":true,"response":"Exactly. Five focused rounds of feedback produced a launch announcement that's exciting, specific, and actionable. The first draft was forgettable. The final version makes people want to click."},{"text":"The first draft was good enough, this was a waste of time","correct":false,"explanation":"Compare them side by side. The first draft would get ignored. The final version has a hook, a pain point, proof, and a CTA. That's the difference between a post that gets 10 views and one that drives signups."},{"text":"I should have written a perfect prompt from the start","correct":false,"explanation":"Nobody writes perfect prompts. Even the best prompt engineers iterate. The skill is knowing WHAT to improve at each step."}]}]}
exercises.explanation: Five rounds, five specific improvements. Each round targeted one weakness: tone, pain point, hook, CTA, and final polish. That's professional iteration.
hints: Each round should fix ONE specific thing — don't try to overhaul everything at once.

lessons.title: Your Toolkit Is Ready
lessons.description: Put it all together — solve a real work scenario with Claude from start to finish.

sections.content: You've learned to prompt, iterate, and pick the right model. Now let's put it all together on a real task — start to finish.
You just turned a vague request into a polished deliverable in 4 prompts. That's Claude in action.
You've just completed your first full Claude workflow — from breaking down the task to delivering a polished output.

You did it. You can prompt, iterate, choose the right model, and solve real problems with Claude. You've got the fundamentals — now let's level up. Next course: The Iteration Game, where you'll master multi-turn conversations and produce polished work every time.

courses.title: Your First Conversation
courses.description: Learn the basics of prompting Claude, understand how conversations work, and avoid common pitfalls like hallucinations.

lessons.title: How Conversations Work
lessons.description: Understand the structure of a Claude conversation and how context works.

sections.content:
Anatomy of a Claude Conversation

Every interaction with Claude follows a simple pattern: you send a message (the prompt), and Claude generates a response (the completion). This back-and-forth continues as a conversation.

Context window

Claude processes your entire conversation history each time it generates a response. This history is called the context window. Think of it as Claude's working memory — everything you have said, everything Claude has said, and any documents or images you have shared.

Claude's context window has a maximum size measured in tokens (roughly 3/4 of a word). Sonnet supports about 200,000 tokens of context — roughly 150,000 words or a 500-page book.

How Claude reads your conversation

Each time you send a message, Claude reads the entire conversation from the beginning. It does not have a special pointer to 'the latest message.' This means:

  • Earlier messages still influence later responses
  • You can reference something you said 50 messages ago
  • If the conversation gets very long, older context may get compressed or dropped

Starting fresh

When you start a new conversation, Claude has zero context. It does not remember your previous conversations. If you need Claude to know something, you must include it in the current conversation.

exercises.question: What is a 'token' in the context of LLMs?
options: A form of payment for API usage
A security authentication key
A roughly word-sized unit that the model uses to process text
A special formatting character
exercises.explanation: Tokens are the basic units LLMs use to process text. One token is roughly 3/4 of a word, so 200,000 tokens is approximately 150,000 words.

exercises.question: When you send a new message in a conversation, what does Claude read?
options: The entire conversation from the beginning
Only your latest message
Only the last 3 messages
A summary of the conversation plus your latest message
exercises.explanation: Claude reads the entire conversation history each time it generates a response. This is why earlier messages continue to influence later responses.

exercises.question: Does Claude remember what you discussed in a previous conversation?
options: Only if the conversations were within the same day
No, each new conversation starts with zero context
Yes, Claude remembers all past conversations
Yes, but only the last conversation
exercises.explanation: Each new conversation starts completely fresh. Claude has no memory of previous conversations unless you explicitly provide that context.

lessons.title: Writing Your First Prompt
lessons.description: Learn the fundamentals of writing clear, effective prompts.

sections.content:
Writing Effective Prompts

A prompt is simply your message to Claude. The quality of Claude's response depends heavily on how you write your prompt. Here are the fundamentals.

Be specific

Vague prompts get vague answers. Instead of 'Tell me about marketing,' try 'Explain 3 low-budget marketing strategies for a new SaaS product targeting small law firms, with estimated costs and timelines.'

Provide context

Claude does not know your situation unless you tell it. Include relevant background: who the audience is, what you have already tried, what format you want, and any constraints.

State the desired output format

Tell Claude exactly what you want back. 'Give me a bullet list,' 'Write this as a professional email,' 'Format the output as a markdown table with columns for name, cost, and timeline.'

One task at a time

When starting out, ask Claude to do one thing per message. 'Summarize this article' is better than 'Summarize this article, then write a tweet thread about it, then create a LinkedIn post.' You can always follow up with additional requests.

Example of a good first prompt

I'm a freelance graphic designer writing a proposal for a corporate client. Write a 200-word project proposal for a brand identity package (logo, color palette, business cards). The tone should be professional but warm. Include a timeline of 3 weeks and a price of $5,000.

This prompt works because it specifies the role, the task, the format, the tone, and the key details.

exercises.question: Which prompt is likely to produce a better response?
options: List the 5 best dog breeds for families with young children, including temperament and size for each
Dogs?
Tell me about dogs
Write something about dogs please
exercises.explanation: Specific prompts get better results. The second option tells Claude exactly what to cover (breeds, audience, attributes), which produces a focused, useful response.

exercises.question: Why is it important to include context in your prompt?
options: Claude does not know your situation unless you tell it
It makes the prompt longer, which costs more money
Without context, Claude will refuse to respond
It is required by Anthropic's terms of service
exercises.explanation: Claude starts each conversation with no knowledge of your specific situation. Providing context (audience, goals, constraints) helps Claude tailor its response to your actual needs.

exercises.question: What does 'state the desired output format' mean?
options: Choose the programming language for code output
Tell Claude whether you want text, a list, a table, an email, etc.
Set the maximum response length in settings
Choose between Opus, Sonnet, or Haiku
exercises.explanation: Telling Claude the desired format (bullet list, email, table, paragraph, etc.) ensures the output matches what you actually need, saving you reformatting time.

lessons.title: Iterating on Responses
lessons.description: Learn how to refine Claude's output through follow-up messages.

sections.content:
The Art of Iteration

Your first prompt rarely produces the perfect response. The real power of Claude comes from iterating — refining the output through follow-up messages.

Give specific feedback

Instead of 'That's not quite right,' tell Claude exactly what to change: 'Make the tone more casual,' 'Remove the third paragraph,' 'Add specific dollar amounts to each recommendation,' or 'Make it half as long.'

Build incrementally

Break complex tasks into steps. Ask Claude to write an outline first, then expand each section. This gives you checkpoints to course-correct before Claude writes too much in the wrong direction.

Use 'more like this, less like that'

If part of the response is good and part is not, tell Claude: 'The first paragraph nailed the tone I want. Rewrite the rest to match that style.' This teaches Claude what you want through example.

Ask Claude to try again differently

You can say: 'Give me 3 alternative versions of that opening line' or 'Try this again, but approach it from the customer's perspective instead.' Claude works well when given permission to explore multiple angles.

Common iteration phrases

  • 'Make this more concise'
  • 'Add more specific examples'
  • 'Change the tone to be more [casual/formal/urgent]'
  • 'Restructure this as [a table/bullet points/numbered steps]'
  • 'Keep the content but simplify the vocabulary for a non-expert audience'

exercises.question: Which follow-up message gives Claude the most useful feedback?
options: That's wrong, try again
Make the tone more casual and cut the word count in half
Better please
I don't like it
exercises.explanation: Specific feedback ('more casual tone' and 'cut word count in half') gives Claude clear direction. Vague feedback like 'try again' or 'I don't like it' forces Claude to guess what you want.

exercises.question: Why is it effective to ask for an outline before a full draft?
options: It gives you a checkpoint to correct direction before Claude writes too much
Outlines use fewer tokens and save money
Claude cannot write long content without an outline
Anthropic recommends this in their documentation
exercises.explanation: Asking for an outline first lets you verify the structure and direction before Claude writes the full content. It is much easier to redirect at the outline stage than after 2,000 words.

exercises.question: What is a good strategy when part of Claude's response is good and part is not?
options: Accept the whole response and edit it yourself
Tell Claude which part was good and ask it to match that style for the rest
Start a completely new conversation
Rate the response with a thumbs down
exercises.explanation: Pointing to what worked ('The first paragraph nailed it, rewrite the rest to match') teaches Claude by example, which is much more effective than starting over.

lessons.title: Understanding Hallucinations
lessons.description: Learn why Claude sometimes makes things up and how to minimize it.

sections.content:
Understanding Hallucinations

Hallucination is when an AI generates content that sounds confident and plausible but is factually incorrect. Every LLM does this, including Claude. Understanding why it happens helps you use Claude more safely.

Why hallucinations happen

LLMs generate text by predicting the most likely next token based on patterns learned during training. They do not 'look things up' from a database of facts. When the model encounters a question where it has limited training data, it fills in the gap with statistically plausible text — which may be wrong.

Where hallucinations are most common

  • Specific facts: Dates, statistics, phone numbers, URLs, legal citations
  • Niche topics: Obscure historical events, minor public figures, small companies
  • Citations: Claude may invent fake academic papers, book titles, or quotes
  • Recent events: Anything after the training data cutoff

How to reduce hallucinations

  • Provide source material: Paste the document, article, or data you want Claude to work with. Claude is much more accurate when working from provided text.
  • Ask Claude to cite its reasoning: 'Explain how you arrived at that number' forces Claude to show its work.
  • Tell Claude it can say 'I don't know': Adding 'If you are not confident, say so' gives Claude permission to be honest about uncertainty.
  • Verify critical facts: For anything that matters (legal, medical, financial), always verify Claude's claims with authoritative sources.

exercises.question: Why do LLMs hallucinate?
options: Their training data contains only incorrect information
Hallucination is a feature, not a bug
They intentionally lie to confuse users
They predict statistically plausible text, which may not be factually correct
exercises.explanation: LLMs generate text by predicting likely next tokens based on training patterns. When they lack sufficient data on a topic, they fill gaps with plausible-sounding but potentially incorrect text.

exercises.question: Which type of content is MOST likely to contain hallucinations?
options: Specific statistics, dates, and citations that Claude generates from memory
Code that follows a well-documented pattern
A summary of a document you pasted into the conversation
A bulleted list reformatting text you provided
exercises.explanation: Specific facts generated from memory (stats, dates, citations) are the most hallucination-prone. When Claude works from text you provide, it is much more accurate.

exercises.question: What is one effective strategy to reduce hallucinations?
options: Use Claude Haiku instead of Sonnet
Ask Claude to respond in a different language
Provide the source material for Claude to work from
Use a longer prompt
exercises.explanation: Providing source material (pasting the document, article, or data) dramatically reduces hallucinations because Claude can work from the provided text rather than relying on its training data.

lessons.title: Claude.ai Tips and Tricks
lessons.description: Master the claude.ai interface with practical tips for everyday use.

sections.content:
Getting the Most from claude.ai

The claude.ai web interface has features that many users miss. Here are practical tips to level up your everyday usage.

Uploading files

You can upload PDFs, images, text files, and code files directly into a conversation. Claude can read and analyze them. This is one of the best ways to reduce hallucinations — give Claude the source material to work from.

Editing your messages

You can edit a previous message and regenerate Claude's response. This is useful when you want to refine your prompt without losing the conversation context.

Copying and formatting

Click the copy button on any Claude response to copy it with formatting intact. For code blocks, there is a dedicated copy button that grabs just the code.

Keyboard shortcuts

Press / to access slash commands. Use Shift + Enter for a new line within your message without sending it. Press Enter to send.

Model switching

You can switch between Claude models (Opus, Sonnet, Haiku) within the same conversation using the model selector. This lets you use Haiku for quick questions and switch to Opus for complex reasoning within the same chat.

Conversation organization

Rename conversations by clicking the title. Use descriptive names so you can find them later. Delete conversations you no longer need to keep your sidebar clean.

exercises.question: Why is uploading source documents to Claude a best practice?
options: It reduces hallucinations by giving Claude real data to work from
It makes Claude respond faster
Uploaded documents are permanently stored for future conversations
It is the only way Claude can read text
exercises.explanation: Uploading source documents gives Claude real data to reference, which dramatically reduces hallucinations compared to asking Claude to rely on its training data alone.

exercises.question: Can you switch between Claude models within the same conversation?
options: Only on the paid plan
No, you must start a new conversation
Only when using the API
Yes, using the model selector
exercises.explanation: You can switch between models within the same conversation using the model selector, allowing you to use the best model for each part of the conversation.

exercises.question: What happens when you edit a previous message in a conversation?
options: A new conversation branch is created
Claude regenerates its response based on the edited message
The conversation is deleted
Nothing, messages cannot be edited
exercises.explanation: Editing a message lets Claude regenerate its response to the updated prompt, which is useful for refining your approach without starting a new conversation.

courses.title: System Prompts & Personas
courses.description: Learn how to use system prompts to control Claude's behavior, create custom personas, and adjust temperature for different tasks.

lessons.title: What Are System Prompts?
lessons.description: Understand the role of system prompts and how they shape Claude's behavior.

sections.content:
System Prompts Explained

A system prompt is a special instruction given to Claude before the conversation begins. It sets the ground rules for how Claude should behave, what role it should play, and what constraints it should follow.

User message vs. system prompt

In a normal conversation, everything you type is a user message. A system prompt is different — it acts as backstage instructions that frame the entire conversation. Think of it like a director's notes to an actor before the show starts.

Where system prompts are used

  • API: Developers pass a system prompt as a separate parameter in the API call
  • Claude.ai Projects: The 'Project Instructions' field acts as a system prompt for all conversations in that project
  • Claude Code: CLAUDE.md files serve as system-level context

What system prompts control

  • Claude's persona and communication style
  • Topics to focus on or avoid
  • Output format requirements
  • Domain-specific knowledge or rules
  • Behavioral guardrails (e.g., 'never give medical advice without a disclaimer')

Example

You are a senior tax accountant. Respond only with information relevant to US federal tax law. Always cite the relevant IRS publication or tax code section. If you are unsure about a specific regulation, say so.

exercises.question: What is a system prompt?
options: The model name (e.g., Sonnet, Opus)
The first message a user types in a conversation
An error message from the system
A special instruction given to Claude before the conversation begins that frames its behavior
exercises.explanation: A system prompt is a backstage instruction that sets the ground rules for Claude's behavior, persona, and constraints before any user messages are exchanged.

exercises.question: On claude.ai, where do you set something that works like a system prompt?
options: In the Project Instructions field within a Project
By typing '/system' before your message
System prompts are only available through the API
In the settings menu under 'Advanced'
exercises.explanation: On claude.ai, the Project Instructions field in Projects functions as a system prompt, applying instructions to all conversations within that project.

exercises.question: Which of these is a good use case for a system prompt?
options: Asking Claude to summarize a specific document
Asking Claude what the weather is today
Telling Claude to always respond as a friendly customer support agent for your SaaS product
Uploading a PDF for analysis
exercises.explanation: System prompts are ideal for persistent behavioral instructions like 'always respond as a customer support agent.' One-off tasks like summarizing a document belong in the user message.

lessons.title: Creating Custom Personas
lessons.description: Design personas that make Claude behave like a specific expert or character.

sections.content:
Designing Effective Personas

A persona is a role you assign to Claude via a system prompt. Well-designed personas dramatically improve response quality because they anchor Claude's behavior to a specific expertise and communication style.

Anatomy of a good persona

  1. Role: Who Claude is. 'You are a senior Python developer with 15 years of experience.'
  2. Expertise: What domain knowledge to draw on. 'You specialize in data pipelines and ETL processes.'
  3. Audience: Who Claude is talking to. 'You are speaking to a junior developer who is new to Python.'
  4. Style: How Claude should communicate. 'Use plain language, avoid jargon, and include code examples.'
  5. Constraints: What to avoid. 'Do not suggest solutions that require paid third-party services.'

Example personas

Technical writer: You are a technical writer for a developer documentation site. Write clear, concise documentation. Use active voice. Include code examples for every concept. Target an audience of intermediate developers.

Business strategist: You are a business strategist advising a B2B SaaS startup at the seed stage. Focus on practical, low-cost strategies. Always consider burn rate and runway in your recommendations. Be direct and skip the fluff.

Combining personas with instructions

The best system prompts combine a persona with specific behavioral rules. For example: persona + output format + topics to avoid + how to handle edge cases.

exercises.question: What are the key components of an effective persona?
options: The model name and temperature setting
Just a job title is enough
Role, expertise, audience, style, and constraints
Name, age, and location
exercises.explanation: An effective persona defines the role, domain expertise, target audience, communication style, and constraints. The more specific these elements are, the better Claude's responses.

exercises.question: Why do personas improve Claude's responses?
options: They anchor Claude's behavior to a specific expertise and communication style
They make Claude respond faster
They are required for the model to work properly
They unlock hidden capabilities in the model
exercises.explanation: Personas help Claude narrow its vast knowledge to a specific domain and adopt an appropriate communication style, resulting in more focused and useful responses.

exercises.question: Which persona prompt is most effective?
options: You are a board-certified cardiologist explaining test results to a patient with no medical background. Use plain language and analogies. Always recommend the patient confirm details with their in-person physician.
You are a medical AI.
Act like a really smart person who knows about health.
Be a doctor.
exercises.explanation: The second option is most effective because it specifies the exact specialty, the audience, the communication style, and a safety constraint (confirm with their doctor).

lessons.title: Temperature and Creativity Controls
lessons.description: Understand how temperature affects Claude's outputs and when to adjust it.

sections.content:
Understanding Temperature

Temperature is a parameter that controls how random or creative Claude's responses are. It ranges from 0 to 1.

Low temperature (0 - 0.3)

Lower temperature means more deterministic, focused responses. Claude picks the most likely tokens, producing consistent, predictable output. Best for:

  • Factual questions
  • Code generation
  • Data extraction
  • Classification tasks
  • Following strict formatting rules

Medium temperature (0.4 - 0.7)

A balance between creativity and consistency. This is the default range. Good for:

  • Writing emails and business content
  • Summarization
  • General Q&A
  • Most everyday tasks

High temperature (0.8 - 1.0)

Higher temperature introduces more randomness. Claude explores less probable tokens, producing more varied, creative output. Best for:

  • Creative writing and brainstorming
  • Generating diverse ideas
  • Exploring unusual angles
  • Poetry and fiction

Where to set temperature

On claude.ai, temperature is managed automatically. Through the API, you pass it as a parameter in your request. In Claude Code, the model configuration handles it. Most users never need to adjust temperature — Claude's defaults work well for general use.

exercises.question: What does a temperature of 0 mean?
options: Claude responds in the most creative way possible
Claude picks the most likely (deterministic) response with minimal randomness
Claude refuses to respond
Claude generates the longest possible response
exercises.explanation: Temperature 0 makes Claude's output maximally deterministic — it picks the most probable next token each time, producing consistent, focused responses.

exercises.question: Which temperature range is best for creative brainstorming?
options: Temperature has no effect on creativity
exercises.explanation: High temperature (0.8-1.0) introduces more randomness, causing Claude to explore less probable tokens and produce more varied, creative outputs.

exercises.question: For code generation, which temperature range is generally best?
options: Temperature does not affect code quality
0.8 - 1.0 for maximum creativity
0 - 0.3 for consistent, deterministic output
Exactly 0.5 always
exercises.explanation: Code generation benefits from low temperature (0-0.3) because you want consistent, correct syntax and logic rather than creative variation.

lessons.title: Behavioral Instructions That Work
lessons.description: Write system prompt instructions that Claude reliably follows.

sections.content:
Writing Instructions Claude Follows

Not all instructions are created equal. Some phrasings reliably control Claude's behavior; others get ignored. Here is what works.

Be explicit and direct

Instead of: 'Try to keep things brief.' Write: 'Keep every response under 100 words.' Claude follows concrete rules better than vague suggestions.

Use positive instructions over negative ones

Instead of: 'Don't use jargon.' Write: 'Use plain language that a high school student could understand.' Telling Claude what TO do is more effective than telling it what NOT to do.

Provide examples

Show Claude what you want: 'When the user asks about pricing, respond in this format: [Feature]: $[price]/month — [one sentence description].' Examples are the single most powerful instruction technique.

Set boundaries for edge cases

Anticipate tricky situations: 'If the user asks about a topic outside your expertise, say: I specialize in tax law and cannot advise on that topic. Please consult an appropriate specialist.'

Priority ordering

Put your most important instructions first. Claude pays more attention to instructions at the beginning of the system prompt. If you have formatting rules, safety rules, and persona instructions, lead with the safety rules.

Test and iterate

System prompts need testing. Send several different types of user messages and check if Claude follows your instructions consistently. Refine anything that is not working.

exercises.question: Which instruction is more likely to be followed reliably?
options: Be concise if possible
Shorter responses are preferred
Try not to be too wordy
Keep every response under 150 words
exercises.explanation: Concrete, measurable instructions like 'under 150 words' are followed much more reliably than vague suggestions like 'try not to be too wordy.'

exercises.question: Why are positive instructions more effective than negative ones?
options: Positive instructions tell Claude what TO do, giving it a clear behavior to follow
Negative instructions cost more tokens
There is no difference in effectiveness
Claude cannot process negative sentences
exercises.explanation: Positive instructions give Claude a clear target behavior. Negative instructions only say what to avoid, leaving Claude to guess what you want instead.

exercises.question: Where should you put your most important instructions in a system prompt?
options: It does not matter
In the middle
At the very end
At the beginning
exercises.explanation: Claude pays more attention to instructions at the beginning of the system prompt. Lead with your most critical rules (safety, core behavior) and put secondary instructions later.

lessons.title: Building a Reusable Prompt Library
lessons.description: Create a personal library of system prompts and personas for repeated use.

sections.content:
Your Personal Prompt Library

Once you create a system prompt that works well, save it. Building a library of tested prompts saves time and ensures consistent quality across your work.

What to save

  • Role-based prompts: 'Tax advisor,' 'Technical writer,' 'Marketing strategist'
  • Task-specific prompts: 'Email editor,' 'Meeting summarizer,' 'Code reviewer'
  • Format templates: 'Blog post structure,' 'Proposal template,' 'Executive brief format'

How to organize

Group prompts by category (writing, analysis, coding, business). Include a brief description of what each prompt does and when to use it. Store them somewhere accessible — a note-taking app, a text file, or claude.ai Projects.

Using Projects as a prompt library

On claude.ai, you can create a Project for each domain (e.g., 'Marketing Writing,' 'Code Reviews'). Set the Project Instructions to your system prompt. Every conversation in that project will use those instructions automatically.

Version your prompts

When you improve a prompt, save both versions. Note what you changed and why. This helps you learn what works and avoids losing a good prompt to a bad edit.

Share and iterate

Prompts are like code — they improve through testing and iteration. If you work on a team, share your best prompts. What works for one person usually works for others.

exercises.question: What is the benefit of saving effective system prompts?
options: It saves time and ensures consistent quality across repeated tasks
It reduces API costs
Claude requires you to reuse prompts
Saved prompts run faster
exercises.explanation: Saving tested system prompts means you do not have to reinvent the wheel each time. You get consistent, high-quality results without spending time crafting new prompts.

exercises.question: How can claude.ai Projects function as a prompt library?
options: Projects store all your previous conversations as prompts
Each Project can have its own system prompt (Project Instructions) that applies to all conversations in that project
Projects are only for file storage
Projects automatically generate prompts for you
exercises.explanation: Each claude.ai Project has a Project Instructions field that acts as a system prompt for every conversation created within that project, making it a natural prompt library.

exercises.question: Why should you version your prompts?
options: Anthropic requires versioning for compliance
Versioning makes prompts run faster
So you can track what changed, learn what works, and avoid losing a good prompt to a bad edit
Each version uses a different Claude model
exercises.explanation: Versioning prompts helps you track improvements, understand what changes were effective, and revert if a new version performs worse.

courses.title: Artifacts & Projects
courses.description: Master claude.ai's Artifacts for creating shareable content and Projects for organizing context across conversations.

lessons.title: Introduction to Artifacts
lessons.description: Learn what Artifacts are and how they enhance your Claude workflow.

sections.content:
What Are Artifacts?

Artifacts are a feature on claude.ai that displays Claude's output in a separate, interactive panel next to the conversation. Instead of code or content appearing inline in the chat, Artifacts render them in a dedicated window where you can view, edit, copy, and share them.

When Claude creates an Artifact

Claude automatically creates an Artifact when it generates certain types of content:

  • Code: Python scripts, JavaScript, HTML, CSS, and other programming languages
  • Documents: Markdown documents, reports, and long-form content
  • Web pages: Interactive HTML with CSS and JavaScript that renders live in the Artifact panel
  • SVGs: Vector graphics and diagrams
  • Mermaid diagrams: Flowcharts, sequence diagrams, and other diagram types
  • React components: Interactive UI components that render live

Why Artifacts matter

Before Artifacts, Claude's code and content were buried in the conversation. You had to scroll through chat history to find that one code block. Artifacts give each piece of substantial content its own persistent, visible space. You can iterate on an Artifact across multiple messages while keeping the conversation focused.

Artifact controls

Each Artifact has controls to copy its content, download it as a file, or share it via a public link. You can also edit the Artifact directly in the panel and ask Claude to modify it.

exercises.question: What is an Artifact in claude.ai?
options: A file stored on Anthropic's servers
A plugin that adds extra capabilities to Claude
A separate interactive panel that displays Claude's generated content (code, documents, web pages)
A conversation template
exercises.explanation: Artifacts display Claude's generated content in a dedicated panel alongside the conversation, making it easy to view, edit, copy, and share code, documents, and interactive content.

exercises.question: Which of these content types can render as an interactive Artifact?
options: Only Markdown documents
Only Python code
HTML with CSS and JavaScript
Only plain text
exercises.explanation: HTML with CSS and JavaScript renders as a live, interactive web page in the Artifact panel, letting you see and interact with the output directly.

exercises.question: What can you do with an Artifact once it is created?
options: Only view it — no other actions
Only download it as a PDF
Only share it with other Claude users
Copy it, download it, share it via a public link, and edit it
exercises.explanation: Artifacts support multiple actions: copying content, downloading as a file, sharing via public link, and direct editing in the panel.

lessons.title: Working with Code Artifacts
lessons.description: Use Artifacts to generate, preview, and iterate on code.

sections.content:
Code Artifacts in Practice

When Claude generates code, it often appears as an Artifact. This gives you a clean view of the code separate from the conversation, making it easier to review, copy, and iterate.

Live preview for web code

If you ask Claude to write HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, the Artifact panel renders the output as a live web page. This means you can see buttons, forms, animations, and interactive elements without needing to copy the code to a separate file. This makes claude.ai a rapid prototyping tool.

Iterating on code Artifacts

You can iterate on a code Artifact across multiple messages. For example:

  1. Ask Claude to create a landing page
  2. See it render in the Artifact panel
  3. Say 'Change the header color to blue and add a signup form'
  4. Claude updates the same Artifact rather than creating a new one

This creates a tight feedback loop where you can visually tweak designs and functionality in real time.

React component Artifacts

Claude can generate React components that render live in the Artifact panel. This is powerful for creating interactive UI prototypes: dashboards, calculators, data visualizations, and more — all without a local development environment.

Downloading code

Use the download button to save any code Artifact as a file. For multi-file projects, you may need to ask Claude to generate each file separately or bundle them together.

exercises.question: What happens when Claude generates an HTML/CSS/JS Artifact?
options: It opens in a new browser tab
It downloads automatically to your computer
It renders as a live, interactive web page in the Artifact panel
It shows only the raw source code
exercises.explanation: HTML/CSS/JavaScript Artifacts render as live web pages in the Artifact panel, letting you see and interact with the output directly without copying code anywhere.

exercises.question: When you ask Claude to modify an existing code Artifact, what happens?
options: Claude creates a brand new Artifact
You must create a new conversation
The original Artifact is deleted
Claude updates the existing Artifact in place
exercises.explanation: Claude updates the existing Artifact rather than creating a new one, maintaining a tight iteration loop where you can refine the same piece of code across multiple messages.

exercises.question: What type of interactive UI prototypes can Claude create as Artifacts?
options: Only static HTML pages
React components with interactive elements like dashboards, calculators, and data visualizations
Only SVG images
Only simple text documents
exercises.explanation: Claude can generate React components that render live in the Artifact panel, enabling the creation of interactive dashboards, calculators, forms, and data visualizations.

lessons.title: Document and Diagram Artifacts
lessons.description: Create documents, reports, and diagrams using Artifacts.

sections.content:
Beyond Code: Documents and Diagrams

Artifacts are not just for code. They are equally powerful for creating structured documents and visual diagrams.

Markdown documents

Ask Claude to write reports, proposals, guides, or any long-form content, and it often creates a Markdown Artifact. The Artifact panel renders the Markdown with proper formatting (headers, bold, lists, tables), giving you a clean preview of the final document.

Mermaid diagrams

Mermaid is a text-based diagramming language that Claude knows well. You can ask Claude to create:

  • Flowcharts: Process flows, decision trees, user journeys
  • Sequence diagrams: API call flows, system interactions
  • Entity relationship diagrams: Database schemas
  • Gantt charts: Project timelines
  • Class diagrams: Object-oriented designs

The diagrams render visually in the Artifact panel. You can iterate on them: 'Add an error handling path to the flowchart' or 'Include the payment service in the sequence diagram.'

SVG graphics

Claude can generate SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) code that renders as crisp vector images in the Artifact panel. This is useful for simple logos, icons, illustrations, and data visualizations.

Combining types

You can ask Claude to create a document Artifact that includes embedded Mermaid diagrams, creating rich technical documentation in a single output.

exercises.question: What is Mermaid in the context of Artifacts?
options: A file format for images
An Anthropic product for design
A text-based diagramming language that renders visual diagrams in the Artifact panel
A Claude model variant
exercises.explanation: Mermaid is a text-based diagramming language that Claude can write. The Artifact panel renders Mermaid code as visual flowcharts, sequence diagrams, Gantt charts, and more.

exercises.question: Which diagram type would you ask Claude to create for visualizing a database schema?
options: Gantt chart
Entity relationship diagram
Pie chart
Sequence diagram
exercises.explanation: Entity relationship (ER) diagrams are specifically designed to visualize database schemas, showing tables, columns, and relationships between tables.

exercises.question: What format does Claude use for generating vector graphics in Artifacts?
options: PNG
SVG
GIF
JPEG
exercises.explanation: Claude generates SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) code, which renders as crisp, scalable vector images in the Artifact panel. SVG is text-based, so Claude can write it directly.

lessons.title: Introduction to Projects
lessons.description: Use Projects to maintain persistent context across conversations.

sections.content:
Claude Projects

Projects are a feature on claude.ai (Pro and Team plans) that let you group conversations around a specific topic or task, with shared context that persists across all conversations in the project.

Why Projects exist

Normally, each Claude conversation starts fresh with no context. If you are working on a recurring task (editing a newsletter, managing a project, coding an app), you have to re-explain your setup every time. Projects solve this by letting you set persistent instructions and upload reference documents that apply to every conversation.

Project components

  • Project Instructions: A text field that works like a system prompt. These instructions apply to every conversation in the project.
  • Project Knowledge: Files you upload to the project. Claude can reference these in any conversation. Upload style guides, documentation, data files, or any reference material.
  • Conversations: All chats within the project share the instructions and knowledge.

Example use cases

  • Content creation: Upload your brand voice guide and set instructions for tone and format
  • Code project: Upload your architecture docs and set coding standards
  • Research: Upload papers and data, set instructions for analysis methodology
  • Client work: One project per client with their specific requirements

Limits

Project Knowledge files count toward your context window. If you upload too many large files, Claude may not be able to process them all in a single conversation. Prioritize the most relevant documents.

exercises.question: What problem do Projects solve?
options: They reduce the cost of API calls
They give you access to more powerful models
They make Claude respond faster
They let you maintain persistent context (instructions and files) across multiple conversations
exercises.explanation: Projects solve the problem of having to re-explain your context every conversation. Project Instructions and Knowledge files persist across all conversations in the project.

exercises.question: What are the two main context components of a Project?
options: Project Instructions and Project Knowledge (uploaded files)
API key and endpoint URL
Username and password
Model name and temperature
exercises.explanation: Projects have two key context components: Project Instructions (persistent system prompt) and Project Knowledge (uploaded reference files), both shared across all conversations.

exercises.question: What should you consider when uploading files to Project Knowledge?
options: Uploaded files count toward the context window, so prioritize the most relevant documents
There is no limit to how many files you can upload
Files are permanently stored and never deleted
Files must be smaller than 1KB
exercises.explanation: Project Knowledge files consume context window space. If you upload too many large files, Claude may not process them all. Upload only the most relevant reference materials.

lessons.title: Advanced Project Workflows
lessons.description: Build powerful workflows combining Projects, Artifacts, and conversations.

sections.content:
Power User Project Workflows

Combining Projects with Artifacts and smart conversation management unlocks workflows that make Claude dramatically more useful for recurring tasks.

The domain expert project

Create a project for each professional domain you work in. Upload your company's style guide, standard templates, and key documents to Project Knowledge. Set Project Instructions to define Claude's persona and rules. Now every conversation in that project starts with Claude already knowing your context.

The iterative document project

For long documents (reports, guides, books), create a project and build the document across multiple conversations. Use one conversation for research, another for outlining, another for writing each section. The Project Knowledge keeps all your reference materials accessible, and you can upload earlier drafts for Claude to build upon.

The code review project

Upload your team's coding standards, architecture decisions, and common patterns to a project. Set instructions like: 'Review code for bugs, security issues, and style violations. Reference our coding standards document when flagging issues.' Every code review conversation starts pre-loaded with your team's standards.

Managing multiple projects

Keep projects focused. A project for 'Marketing' is too broad. 'Q2 Product Launch Campaign' is better. When a project's purpose is complete, archive it. Create new projects for new initiatives rather than overloading existing ones.

Combining with Artifacts

Within a project, Artifacts persist across the conversation. You can create a document Artifact in one message, refine it in the next, and share the final version — all with the project's context guiding Claude's behavior.

exercises.question: What makes a well-scoped Project?
options: Containing only one conversation
As broad as possible to cover all topics
Focused on a specific initiative or domain with clear purpose
Using only the most expensive model
exercises.explanation: Well-scoped projects are focused on a specific initiative (e.g., 'Q2 Product Launch') rather than broad categories (e.g., 'Marketing'). This keeps instructions and knowledge relevant.

exercises.question: How can you use a Project for code reviews?
options: Create a new project for every single code review
Only by using the API
Projects cannot be used for code
Upload coding standards and architecture docs to Knowledge, set review instructions, and use it for all code review conversations
exercises.explanation: Uploading team standards and setting review-focused instructions creates a reusable environment where every code review conversation starts with Claude knowing your team's standards.

exercises.question: When should you archive a Project and create a new one?
options: Only when you run out of storage
Never, keep adding to the same project forever
After every conversation
When the project's purpose is complete or the scope has shifted significantly
exercises.explanation: Archive projects when their purpose is complete. Create new projects for new initiatives rather than overloading existing ones with unrelated context.

courses.title: Claude for Writing
courses.description: Use Claude as a writing partner for emails, long-form content, editing, and finding the right tone for any audience.

lessons.title: Writing Professional Emails
lessons.description: Use Claude to draft, edit, and refine professional emails.

sections.content:
Claude as Your Email Writing Partner

Email is where most professionals spend significant time. Claude can draft emails from scratch, rewrite awkward drafts, and adjust tone — saving you hours per week.

Drafting from scratch

Give Claude the context it needs: who you are writing to, what you want to communicate, what tone is appropriate, and any constraints. Example:

Write a professional email to my client Sarah Chen at Acme Corp. I need to inform her that the project deadline is moving from March 15 to April 1 due to a supply chain delay. Tone: apologetic but confident. Include a revised timeline and reassure her that quality will not be affected.

Rewriting existing drafts

Paste your draft and ask Claude to improve it: 'Here is my draft email. Make it more concise, fix any grammar issues, and make the tone more professional.' Claude can also adjust formality levels: 'Rewrite this to be more casual and friendly.'

Email reply assistance

Paste an email you received and ask Claude to draft a reply: 'Here is an email from a potential customer asking about pricing. Draft a reply that presents our three pricing tiers, highlights the mid-tier value, and invites them to a demo call.'

Cold outreach

Claude can write cold emails, but be specific about the recipient and value proposition. Generic cold emails fail. Tell Claude: the recipient's role, their likely pain points, your offer, and the desired call to action.

exercises.question: What context should you provide when asking Claude to draft an email?
options: A template from the internet
The recipient, the message, the desired tone, and any constraints
Only the email address
Just the subject line
exercises.explanation: Providing the recipient, your message, the tone, and any constraints gives Claude everything it needs to write an email that matches your specific situation.

exercises.question: What is the best way to have Claude improve an existing email draft?
options: Describe the email from memory
Paste the actual draft and give specific feedback on what to change
Start a new conversation and try again
Ask Claude to guess what your email said
exercises.explanation: Pasting the actual draft with specific improvement requests (more concise, different tone, fix grammar) gives Claude concrete material to work with and clear direction.

exercises.question: Why do generic cold emails typically fail?
options: Cold emails always go to spam
Email clients block AI-generated content
They lack specificity about the recipient's pain points and a clear value proposition
Claude cannot write cold emails
exercises.explanation: Generic cold emails fail because they do not address the recipient's specific situation. Effective cold emails require specificity about the person's role, pain points, and a tailored value proposition.

lessons.title: Long-Form Content Creation
lessons.description: Use Claude to write blog posts, articles, reports, and other long content.

sections.content:
Writing Long-Form Content with Claude

Claude excels at long-form content but requires a different approach than short-form writing. The key is working in stages.

Stage 1: Brief and outline

Start by telling Claude what you want to write and asking for an outline: 'I'm writing a 2,000-word blog post about how small law firms can use AI to automate intake. Target audience: managing partners at 5-20 attorney firms. Give me a detailed outline with section headings and key points for each section.'

Review and refine the outline before moving on.

Stage 2: Section-by-section drafting

Write one section at a time: 'Now write Section 2: Current Pain Points in Client Intake. Include specific examples and statistics.' This gives you control over each section and prevents Claude from going in the wrong direction for 2,000 words.

Stage 3: Assembly and polish

Once all sections are drafted, ask Claude to review the full piece: 'Here is the complete draft. Check for consistency in tone, smooth the transitions between sections, and add a compelling opening hook.'

Tips for better long-form content

  • Provide example pieces you like: 'Write in a style similar to this article: [paste excerpt]'
  • Specify word counts per section to control length
  • Include your unique insights and data — Claude provides structure, you provide substance
  • Ask for multiple headline options and pick the best one

exercises.question: Why should you write long-form content in stages rather than asking for the entire piece at once?
options: Long content always requires the API
It gives you checkpoints to course-correct before Claude writes too much in the wrong direction
Stages cost fewer tokens
Claude cannot generate more than 500 words at a time
exercises.explanation: Working in stages (outline, then section-by-section) lets you verify direction and tone at each step, preventing a 2,000-word piece that misses the mark entirely.

exercises.question: What is the purpose of providing an example piece you like when asking Claude to write content?
options: It helps Claude match the style, tone, and structure you prefer
Claude needs reference material for all writing tasks
Claude will copy it word for word
It replaces the need for any other instructions
exercises.explanation: Providing an example gives Claude a concrete reference for the writing style, tone, and structure you want, which is more effective than trying to describe your preferred style in words.

exercises.question: What should you do after Claude drafts all sections of a long piece?
options: Publish it immediately
Delete it and start over
Nothing, section-by-section drafts are always perfect
Ask Claude to review the full draft for consistency, transitions, and a strong opening
exercises.explanation: After assembling sections, a final review pass checks for consistent tone, smooth transitions between sections, and overall coherence that individual section drafting may miss.

lessons.title: Editing and Proofreading
lessons.description: Use Claude as an editor to catch errors and improve your writing.

sections.content:
Claude as Your Editor

Claude is an excellent writing editor. It can catch grammar errors, improve clarity, tighten prose, and suggest structural improvements — all while maintaining your voice.

Types of editing

  • Proofreading: 'Fix any grammar, spelling, and punctuation errors in this text.' Pure error correction.
  • Copy editing: 'Edit for clarity, conciseness, and grammar. Remove redundant phrases and simplify complex sentences.' Improves readability.
  • Substantive editing: 'Review this for logical flow, argument strength, and structure. Suggest reorganization if needed.' Addresses content and organization.
  • Style editing: 'Edit this to match [specific style]. Keep the content but adjust the voice.' Changes tone and style.

Preserving your voice

A common concern is that Claude will 'sound like AI.' To prevent this: 'Edit this to improve clarity and grammar, but keep my natural writing voice. Do not make it sound more formal or corporate. Preserve my casual tone.'

Track changes approach

Ask Claude to show its edits: 'Edit this paragraph and explain each change you made.' This helps you learn from Claude's edits and decide which changes to accept.

Batch editing

For longer documents, paste the whole text and ask for a specific type of edit. Claude can handle editing documents up to tens of thousands of words in a single pass.

exercises.question: What is the difference between proofreading and substantive editing?
options: Proofreading is free; substantive editing requires a paid plan
There is no difference
Proofreading fixes errors; substantive editing addresses content, logic, and structure
Proofreading is for long documents; substantive editing is for short ones
exercises.explanation: Proofreading focuses on surface errors (grammar, spelling, punctuation). Substantive editing goes deeper, evaluating logical flow, argument strength, and whether the content should be reorganized.

exercises.question: How can you prevent Claude from making your writing sound generic or 'AI-like' during editing?
options: Use a lower temperature
Use Claude Haiku instead of Sonnet
Only edit one sentence at a time
Explicitly instruct Claude to preserve your natural voice and tone
exercises.explanation: Explicitly telling Claude to preserve your voice ('keep my casual tone,' 'don't make it more formal') prevents Claude from defaulting to a generic, polished style.

exercises.question: Why is asking Claude to explain its edits useful?
options: It reduces the number of errors
It helps you learn from the edits and decide which changes to accept
It makes Claude edit more carefully
It is required by Anthropic's terms of service
exercises.explanation: When Claude explains each edit, you understand the reasoning behind changes, learn to avoid those errors in the future, and can selectively accept or reject specific edits.

lessons.title: Tone and Voice Control
lessons.description: Master the art of controlling Claude's writing tone for any audience.

sections.content:
Controlling Tone and Voice

Tone is the emotional quality of writing — formal, casual, urgent, playful, authoritative. Voice is the personality behind the writing. Claude can produce a wide range of both, but you need to be specific.

Tone spectrum examples

  • Formal: 'We are pleased to inform you that your application has been approved.' (Corporate, legal)
  • Professional: 'Your application has been approved. Here are the next steps.' (Business communication)
  • Conversational: 'Great news — you're approved! Here's what happens next.' (Blog, newsletter)
  • Casual: 'You're in! Let me walk you through what's next.' (Social media, startup)

Specifying tone effectively

Don't just say 'professional.' Say: 'Write like the Harvard Business Review — authoritative but accessible, data-driven, and direct.' Or: 'Write like a friend who happens to be an expert explaining this over coffee.'

Audience-driven tone

The best approach is to describe your audience: 'The reader is a busy CEO who skims. Use short paragraphs, bold key takeaways, and lead with the bottom line.' Claude will adapt tone, vocabulary, and structure to match.

Maintaining consistency

For ongoing projects, capture your tone preferences in a system prompt or Project Instructions: 'Always write in a warm, direct tone. Use contractions. Avoid jargon. Read level: 8th grade. Sentences max 20 words.'

Tone translation

Claude can also translate tone: 'Here is a formal legal notice. Rewrite it as a friendly email to customers that communicates the same information without the legal jargon.'

exercises.question: Which tone instruction is most effective?
options: Be professional
Write like the Harvard Business Review — authoritative but accessible, data-driven, and direct
Make it sound nice
Good tone please
exercises.explanation: Referencing a specific publication or style ('like the Harvard Business Review') gives Claude a concrete model to emulate, which is far more effective than vague instructions.

exercises.question: What is the most effective way to get Claude to adjust its writing for a specific audience?
options: Ask Claude to use bigger words
Upload a photo of the audience
Change the model to Haiku
Describe the audience in detail: their role, reading habits, and what they care about
exercises.explanation: Describing the audience (busy CEO who skims, technical engineer who wants details, etc.) lets Claude adapt vocabulary, structure, and depth to match their expectations.

exercises.question: How can you maintain consistent tone across multiple conversations?
options: Use the same greeting in every message
Always use the same Claude model
It is impossible to maintain consistency
Capture tone preferences in Project Instructions or a system prompt
exercises.explanation: Capturing tone rules in Project Instructions or a system prompt ensures that every conversation starts with the same tone guidelines, producing consistent output.

lessons.title: Specialized Writing Tasks
lessons.description: Use Claude for resumes, cover letters, social media, and other specific writing tasks.

sections.content:
Specialized Writing with Claude

Beyond emails and articles, Claude handles many specialized writing formats. Here are techniques for common requests.

Resumes and cover letters

Provide: your current role, target role, key achievements (with numbers), and the job description. Ask Claude to tailor the content to the specific position: 'Here is my experience and the job description. Write a one-page resume that highlights the most relevant experience for this role. Quantify achievements where possible.'

Social media posts

Specify the platform, character limits, and goal: 'Write a LinkedIn post about our new product launch. Max 1,300 characters. Hook in the first line. Include a call to action. Professional but energetic tone.' Each platform has different norms — tell Claude which one.

Executive summaries

For reports and proposals: 'Write a 200-word executive summary of this 10-page report. Lead with the key recommendation, then supporting evidence, then next steps. Write for a C-suite audience that will spend 30 seconds reading this.'

Legal and compliance writing

Claude can draft terms of service, privacy policies, and disclaimers, but always have a lawyer review them. Claude is a drafting tool for legal content, not a replacement for legal counsel.

Translation and localization

Claude translates between major languages and can localize content: 'Translate this product description to Spanish (Mexico). Adapt any idioms or cultural references for a Mexican audience.'

exercises.question: When asking Claude to write a resume, what should you provide?
options: A generic resume template
Your experience, the target role, key achievements with numbers, and the job description
Only your name and email
Only the company name
