exercises.explanation: Claude needs your experience, the target role, quantified achievements, and the specific job description to tailor a resume that highlights the most relevant qualifications.

exercises.question: Should you publish Claude-generated legal documents (terms of service, privacy policies) without review?
options: Yes, if you use the right system prompt
Yes, Claude is trained on legal data
No, always have a lawyer review legal content — Claude is a drafting tool, not legal counsel
Only if you use Claude Opus
exercises.explanation: Claude can draft legal documents effectively, but they must be reviewed by qualified legal counsel. Claude is a drafting assistant, not a substitute for a lawyer.

exercises.question: Why is it important to specify the platform when asking Claude to write social media content?
options: Claude can only write for one platform at a time
Different platforms charge different rates
Social media platforms require special API access
Each platform has different character limits, norms, and audience expectations
exercises.explanation: LinkedIn, X (Twitter), Instagram, and other platforms have different character limits, content norms, and audience expectations. Specifying the platform helps Claude match the format.

courses.title: Claude for Research
courses.description: Use Claude to analyze documents, summarize information, cross-reference sources, and build structured research workflows.

lessons.title: Summarization Techniques
lessons.description: Master different summarization approaches for different needs.

sections.content:
Summarization with Claude

Summarization is one of Claude's strongest capabilities. But 'summarize this' is a weak prompt. Different situations require different summarization strategies.

Types of summaries

  • Executive summary: Key findings and recommendations in 2-3 paragraphs for decision-makers
  • Key points extraction: Bullet list of the 5-10 most important facts or arguments
  • Progressive summary: One-sentence, one-paragraph, and one-page versions of the same content
  • Topical summary: Summarize only the parts relevant to a specific topic or question

Effective summarization prompts

'Summarize this 20-page report in 5 bullet points. Each bullet should be one sentence. Focus on findings that affect our Q2 budget decision.'

'Read this legal document and extract: (1) the key obligations for each party, (2) the termination conditions, and (3) any liability caps or limitations.'

Maintaining accuracy

Always provide the source document. Claude is far more accurate when summarizing text you provide versus recalling information from training data. For critical summaries, ask Claude to quote the relevant source text alongside its summary.

Handling long documents

Claude's large context window means you can paste very long documents directly. For documents that exceed the context limit, break them into sections and summarize each, then ask Claude to synthesize the section summaries into a coherent overall summary.

exercises.question: Why is 'summarize this' a weak prompt?
options: Summaries are not a supported feature
It uses too few tokens
It does not specify the length, format, audience, or focus area for the summary
Claude does not understand the word 'summarize'
exercises.explanation: Without specifying length, format, audience, or which aspects to focus on, Claude has to guess what kind of summary you need, often producing something too generic to be useful.

exercises.question: What is a 'progressive summary'?
options: A summary that adds new information
A summary that gets worse over time
Multiple versions at different lengths (one sentence, one paragraph, one page) of the same content
A summary written in a progressive political style
exercises.explanation: A progressive summary provides the same content at multiple detail levels: a one-liner for quick reference, a paragraph for moderate detail, and a full page for comprehensive understanding.

exercises.question: How should you handle a document that exceeds Claude's context window?
options: Use a different AI tool
Break it into sections, summarize each, then synthesize the section summaries
Upload it as a PDF — PDFs have no size limit
It is impossible to summarize
exercises.explanation: For very long documents, break them into sections that fit the context window, summarize each section, then ask Claude to combine the section summaries into a coherent overall summary.

lessons.title: Document Analysis
lessons.description: Extract insights, compare documents, and analyze data with Claude.

sections.content:
Analyzing Documents with Claude

Claude can do much more than summarize. It can extract structured data, compare documents, identify patterns, and answer specific questions about content.

Structured extraction

Turn unstructured documents into structured data: 'Read this contract and extract the following into a table: party names, effective date, term length, payment terms, termination clause, and governing law.'

This works for invoices, reports, emails, legal documents, medical records, and any other semi-structured text.

Comparative analysis

Upload two or more documents and ask Claude to compare them: 'Here are two vendor proposals. Create a comparison table covering price, delivery timeline, warranty terms, and support options. Highlight the key differences and recommend which vendor to choose based on value.'

Pattern identification

Give Claude a set of data and ask it to find patterns: 'Here are 50 customer support tickets from this month. Identify the top 5 recurring issues, their frequency, and suggest process improvements for each.'

Question answering over documents

Upload a document and ask specific questions: 'Based on this quarterly earnings report, what was the year-over-year revenue growth? What segments drove the growth? What risks did management highlight?'

Multi-document synthesis

Upload several related documents and ask Claude to synthesize: 'Here are 5 articles about renewable energy trends. Synthesize the key themes, note where the sources agree and disagree, and identify gaps in coverage.'

exercises.question: What does 'structured extraction' mean when analyzing documents?
options: Printing the document
Converting unstructured text into organized, structured data (tables, lists, key-value pairs)
Deleting the document structure
Translating the document to another language
exercises.explanation: Structured extraction takes unstructured text (contracts, reports, emails) and pulls out specific data points into an organized format like a table or key-value list.

exercises.question: How can Claude help with comparative analysis?
options: Comparative analysis requires the API
Upload multiple documents and ask Claude to compare specific dimensions and highlight differences
Claude can only analyze one document at a time
Claude automatically compares all documents in your account
exercises.explanation: You can upload multiple documents and ask Claude to compare them along specific dimensions (price, terms, quality), highlight differences, and make recommendations.

exercises.question: What is multi-document synthesis?
options: Combining insights from multiple documents to identify themes, agreements, disagreements, and gaps
Translating multiple documents simultaneously
Deleting duplicate documents
Merging documents into one file
exercises.explanation: Multi-document synthesis involves Claude reading several related documents and combining insights to identify common themes, points of agreement and disagreement, and gaps in coverage.

lessons.title: Fact-Checking and Verification
lessons.description: Use Claude to verify claims and build a verification workflow.

sections.content:
Fact-Checking with Claude

Claude can help you evaluate claims, identify logical fallacies, and build verification workflows — but it is essential to understand the limits of AI-assisted fact-checking.

What Claude can verify

  • Logical consistency: 'Does this argument contain any logical fallacies?'
  • Internal consistency: 'Do the numbers in this report add up? Check the totals against the line items.'
  • Claims against provided sources: 'Here is an article and the study it cites. Does the article accurately represent the study's findings?'
  • General knowledge: Claude can flag claims that contradict widely established facts from its training data

What Claude cannot reliably verify

  • Very recent events: Anything after the training data cutoff
  • Niche statistics: Specific numbers that Claude may not have seen in training
  • Evolving topics: Laws, regulations, and policies that change frequently

Building a verification workflow

  1. Ask Claude to identify all factual claims in a document
  2. Ask Claude to rate its confidence in each claim (high, medium, low)
  3. For low-confidence claims, Claude flags them for manual verification
  4. You verify the flagged claims with authoritative sources

This workflow uses Claude's strengths (fast analysis, logical checking) while acknowledging its limits (no real-time web access, potential hallucination).

exercises.question: Which fact-checking task is Claude most reliable at?
options: Confirming the current weather
Checking logical consistency and whether an article accurately represents its cited source
Verifying yesterday's stock prices
Verifying real-time sports scores
exercises.explanation: Claude excels at logical analysis and checking whether claims in one document are consistent with claims in another provided document. It cannot verify real-time data.

exercises.question: In a verification workflow, what should you do with claims Claude rates as 'low confidence'?
options: Verify them manually with authoritative sources
Ask Claude to increase its confidence
Accept them anyway
Delete them from the document
exercises.explanation: Low-confidence claims should be verified manually with authoritative sources. Claude's role is to flag what needs human verification, not to be the final authority.

exercises.question: Why can't Claude reliably fact-check very recent events?
options: Claude is only trained on historical data
Recent events are classified information
Claude does not understand current events
Claude's knowledge has a training data cutoff and it does not browse the internet by default
exercises.explanation: Claude's knowledge comes from training data with a cutoff date. Without real-time web access, it cannot verify events or data that occurred after its training cutoff.

lessons.title: Research Frameworks
lessons.description: Apply structured research methodologies with Claude's help.

sections.content:
Structured Research with Claude

Claude works best for research when you give it a framework to follow rather than open-ended questions. Here are proven research structures.

MECE analysis (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive)

'Break down the US electric vehicle market into MECE categories. For each category, provide market size estimate, growth rate, key players, and trends.' This ensures comprehensive coverage without overlap.

SWOT analysis

'Perform a SWOT analysis of [Company] entering the [Market]. Be specific: name competitors, cite market data from the document I uploaded, and provide actionable recommendations for each quadrant.'

Literature review structure

For academic or professional research: 'I am researching [topic]. Here are 8 papers. For each paper, extract: (1) research question, (2) methodology, (3) key findings, (4) limitations. Then identify themes across all papers and gaps in the existing research.'

Competitive analysis framework

'Analyze these 5 competitors across: pricing model, target customer, key features, market positioning, and recent product changes. Present as a comparison matrix and identify our differentiation opportunities.'

The 5 Whys

For root cause analysis: 'A customer churned after 3 months. Apply the 5 Whys framework to identify potential root causes, going 5 levels deep for each initial hypothesis.'

Combining frameworks

You can chain frameworks: start with MECE segmentation, then SWOT each segment, then competitive analysis within the most promising segment.

exercises.question: What does MECE stand for?
options: Most Efficient Cost Estimate
Market Entry Competitive Evaluation
Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive
Managed Enterprise Content Engine
exercises.explanation: MECE (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive) is a framework for breaking down a topic into categories that do not overlap and together cover the entire scope.

exercises.question: Why do research frameworks improve Claude's output?
options: Frameworks are required by the API
They make Claude respond faster
They provide structure that ensures comprehensive, organized analysis instead of vague responses
They reduce token usage
exercises.explanation: Frameworks give Claude a clear structure to follow, ensuring comprehensive coverage and organized output instead of the unfocused responses that open-ended questions often produce.

exercises.question: What is the '5 Whys' framework used for?
options: Root cause analysis — iteratively asking 'why' to drill down to the fundamental cause of a problem
Creating 5 versions of a document
Asking 5 different questions about a topic
Evaluating 5 competitors
exercises.explanation: The 5 Whys is a root cause analysis technique where you repeatedly ask 'why' to drill past surface symptoms to the fundamental underlying cause of a problem.

lessons.title: Building a Research Pipeline
lessons.description: Create end-to-end research workflows that combine multiple Claude techniques.

sections.content:
End-to-End Research Pipelines

The most powerful use of Claude for research is building a multi-step pipeline where each step feeds into the next.

Example pipeline: Market research

  1. Gather: Upload industry reports, competitor websites, and market data to a Project
  2. Extract: Ask Claude to extract key data points from each source into a structured format
  3. Analyze: Ask Claude to identify patterns, trends, and anomalies across the extracted data
  4. Synthesize: Ask Claude to combine findings into a coherent narrative with supporting evidence
  5. Verify: Ask Claude to flag any claims it is less confident about for manual verification
  6. Present: Ask Claude to format the findings as an executive brief, a presentation outline, or a full report

Using Projects for research pipelines

Create a dedicated Project for each research effort. Upload all source materials to Project Knowledge. Set Project Instructions to define your research methodology and output requirements. Run each pipeline step as a separate conversation within the project.

Maintaining source integrity

Ask Claude to tag every finding with its source document: 'For each insight, note which uploaded document it came from and the relevant section.' This makes your research traceable and verifiable.

Iterative deepening

Start broad and narrow down. First pass: high-level themes. Second pass: deep dive into the most relevant themes. Third pass: specific data points and evidence for your key arguments.

exercises.question: What is the advantage of a multi-step research pipeline over a single prompt?
options: It uses fewer tokens
It is faster
Each step can be reviewed and refined before feeding into the next, producing higher quality output
Claude requires multiple steps for all research
exercises.explanation: A multi-step pipeline gives you checkpoints at each stage (extraction, analysis, synthesis). You can verify and refine at each step, preventing errors from compounding.

exercises.question: Why should you ask Claude to tag findings with their source documents?
options: Tags help Claude process documents faster
It makes the output longer
It makes the research traceable and verifiable
Claude requires source tags to function
exercises.explanation: Source tagging creates an audit trail, letting you verify each finding against its original document. This is essential for credible research.

exercises.question: What is 'iterative deepening' in a research workflow?
options: Making Claude's responses progressively longer
Repeating the same prompt multiple times
Starting with broad themes and narrowing down to specific evidence across multiple passes
Using increasingly powerful Claude models
exercises.explanation: Iterative deepening starts with a broad first pass (high-level themes), then progressively narrows to specific data points and evidence for your key arguments across subsequent passes.

courses.title: Claude for Problem-Solving
courses.description: Use Claude as a thinking partner for brainstorming, decision-making, and working through complex problems.

lessons.title: Brainstorming with Claude
lessons.description: Use Claude to generate ideas, explore angles, and push past creative blocks.

sections.content:
Claude as a Brainstorming Partner

Claude is an exceptional brainstorming partner because it has no ego, no fear of bad ideas, and infinite patience. It can generate many ideas quickly and approach problems from angles you might not consider.

Quantity-first brainstorming

Start by asking for volume: 'Give me 20 ideas for reducing customer churn in a B2B SaaS product. Include wild ideas I would not normally consider.' Quantity first, filter later. Claude's willingness to suggest unconventional ideas is a strength.

Constraint-based brainstorming

Add constraints to force creativity: 'Give me 10 marketing ideas that cost less than $100 each' or 'How could we solve this if we had no engineering resources?' Constraints prevent generic answers.

Perspective shifting

Ask Claude to brainstorm from different viewpoints: 'How would a customer see this problem? How would a competitor exploit it? How would a venture capitalist evaluate it? How would a user who hates technology approach it?'

Building on ideas

Pick the best ideas from Claude's list and go deeper: 'Expand on ideas 3, 7, and 12. For each, give me: implementation steps, estimated cost, timeline, and potential risks.' This turns raw ideas into actionable plans.

Devil's advocate

Ask Claude to poke holes in your ideas: 'Here is my plan. Act as a harsh critic. What are the weakest points? What could go wrong? What am I not seeing?' Claude is particularly good at identifying blind spots you might have.

exercises.question: Why is asking for a large quantity of ideas a good brainstorming technique?
options: Volume prevents Claude from self-filtering and increases the chance of finding unexpected, valuable ideas
Claude can only brainstorm in large batches
More ideas cost fewer tokens per idea
It makes the conversation longer
exercises.explanation: Asking for many ideas (20+) prevents Claude from self-filtering to only 'safe' suggestions. The unconventional ideas that slip through are often the most valuable.

exercises.question: What is 'constraint-based brainstorming'?
options: Only brainstorming about constraints
Limiting Claude to short responses
Using Claude Haiku for brainstorming to save costs
Adding specific limitations (budget, resources, time) that force more creative, targeted ideas
exercises.explanation: Adding constraints like budget limits, time pressure, or resource restrictions forces Claude to generate more creative, practical ideas instead of generic suggestions.

exercises.question: How can the 'devil's advocate' technique help improve your plans?
options: It makes Claude argue with you
It is a way to restart the conversation
Claude identifies weaknesses, risks, and blind spots in your plan that you might miss
It tests whether Claude understands your plan
exercises.explanation: The devil's advocate technique leverages Claude's ability to analyze plans from multiple angles, identifying weaknesses and blind spots that you might overlook due to familiarity bias.

lessons.title: Decision Frameworks
lessons.description: Use structured decision frameworks with Claude's help.

sections.content:
Making Better Decisions with Claude

Claude is a powerful decision-support tool when you give it structured frameworks to work within.

Pros and cons with weights

Go beyond simple pro/con lists: 'I'm deciding between Option A and Option B. List pros and cons for each. Weight each factor from 1-10 by importance to my situation. Calculate a weighted score.' This quantifies what is usually a gut feeling.

Decision matrix

'I'm evaluating 4 CRM platforms. Create a decision matrix with these criteria: price, ease of use, integrations, support quality, and scalability. Rate each on 1-5 for each criterion. Weight the criteria based on the context I provided about my business.'

Pre-mortem analysis

Before committing to a decision: 'Imagine we chose Option A and it failed completely 6 months from now. Write the story of what went wrong. What were the warning signs we ignored?' This surfaces risks that optimism bias hides.

Second-order thinking

'If we make this decision, what happens next? And what happens after that? Trace the chain of consequences 3-4 steps out.' Most people only think about first-order effects. Claude can map the downstream implications.

Reversibility check

Ask Claude: 'Is this decision easily reversible? What would it cost (in time, money, reputation) to reverse it in 6 months?' Irreversible decisions deserve more analysis; reversible ones deserve speed.

exercises.question: What advantage does a weighted pros/cons list have over a simple pros/cons list?
options: Weighted lists are required for business decisions
It quantifies the relative importance of each factor, turning gut feelings into structured analysis
It is longer
It only shows the positives
exercises.explanation: Weighting factors by importance turns a subjective exercise into a more structured analysis, preventing less important factors from overshadowing critical ones.

exercises.question: What is a 'pre-mortem' analysis?
options: Analyzing what went wrong after a project fails
Imagining the decision has already failed and working backward to identify what went wrong
A medical analysis technique
Testing a product before launch
exercises.explanation: A pre-mortem imagines the decision has already failed in the future and asks 'what went wrong?' This surfaces risks and warning signs that optimism bias normally hides.

exercises.question: What is 'second-order thinking'?
options: Making a decision twice
Using Claude's second-best model
Getting a second opinion from another person
Tracing the chain of consequences 3-4 steps beyond the immediate, first-order effects
exercises.explanation: Second-order thinking looks beyond the immediate effects of a decision to the downstream consequences: 'If this happens, then what? And then what?' Most people only consider first-order effects.

lessons.title: Problem Decomposition
lessons.description: Break complex problems into manageable, solvable parts.

sections.content:
Breaking Down Complex Problems

The most common mistake with Claude is asking it to solve a huge, complex problem in one shot. Complex problems need decomposition — breaking them into smaller, manageable sub-problems.

Why decomposition works

Claude handles specific, focused questions much better than vague, sprawling ones. Breaking 'How do I grow my business?' into 10 specific sub-questions produces vastly better answers than asking the big question directly.

Step 1: Map the problem

Ask Claude to help decompose: 'I want to launch an online course. Break this goal into all the sub-tasks and decisions I need to address, organized by phase (planning, creation, launch, post-launch).'

Step 2: Identify dependencies

'For these sub-tasks, which ones depend on others? Which can run in parallel? Create a dependency map.' This reveals the critical path.

Step 3: Solve each sub-problem

Address each sub-problem in a focused message or conversation. 'For pricing: given a 10-hour course targeting mid-career professionals, what pricing models should I consider? Analyze the tradeoffs of each.'

Step 4: Reassemble

'Here are the solutions for each sub-problem. Now synthesize them into a coherent action plan with timeline and priorities.'

Recursive decomposition

If a sub-problem is still too complex, decompose it further. Keep breaking down until each piece is small enough to solve clearly.

exercises.question: Why is asking Claude to solve a huge, complex problem in one shot a mistake?
options: Claude will refuse to answer complex questions
Complex problems cost more tokens
Claude has a character limit
Claude handles specific, focused questions much better than vague, sprawling ones
exercises.explanation: Claude produces much better answers for specific, focused questions. Breaking a big problem into sub-problems lets Claude give thorough, actionable answers to each piece.

exercises.question: What is the purpose of identifying dependencies between sub-tasks?
options: To reduce the number of tasks
To reveal the critical path and which tasks can run in parallel
To make the project take longer
Dependencies are only relevant for coding
exercises.explanation: Identifying dependencies reveals which tasks must happen sequentially (the critical path) and which can run in parallel, enabling more efficient planning.

exercises.question: What should you do if a sub-problem is still too complex to solve clearly?
options: Skip it and move to the next task
Give up on that part
Ask Claude to solve it anyway
Decompose it further into smaller sub-problems (recursive decomposition)
exercises.explanation: If a sub-problem is still too complex, break it down further. Keep decomposing recursively until each piece is small enough for Claude to address clearly and thoroughly.

lessons.title: Scenario Planning
lessons.description: Use Claude to model different scenarios and prepare for uncertainty.

sections.content:
Scenario Planning with Claude

The future is uncertain, but you can prepare for multiple possibilities. Claude is an excellent scenario planning partner because it can quickly generate detailed, internally consistent scenarios.

Best/Likely/Worst case scenarios

'We are launching a new product next quarter. Create three scenarios — best case, most likely, and worst case — for the first 6 months. For each scenario, include: revenue projections, customer acquisition numbers, key risks, and the trigger events that would signal we are in that scenario.'

What-if analysis

Explore specific variables: 'What happens if our main competitor drops their price by 30%? Model the impact on our market share, revenue, and what strategic options we would have.' Claude can trace the implications of a specific change through your business.

Contingency planning

For each risk: 'For our worst-case scenario, create a contingency plan. What early warning signs should we watch for? What actions should we take immediately? What resources do we need on standby?'

Opportunity mapping

Scenarios are not just about risks: 'If our best-case scenario materializes, what opportunities does that create? How should we prepare now to capitalize on upside potential?'

Stress testing plans

'Here is our business plan. Stress test it against these scenarios: (1) a recession hits, (2) our main supplier goes bankrupt, (3) a new regulation bans our core feature. How does our plan hold up? Where does it break?'

exercises.question: What is the purpose of best/likely/worst case scenario planning?
options: To convince investors
To choose the most optimistic option
To predict the future accurately
To prepare for multiple possible outcomes and identify trigger events that signal which scenario is unfolding
exercises.explanation: Scenario planning prepares you for multiple possible futures. The key is identifying trigger events that signal which scenario is materializing, so you can respond proactively.

exercises.question: What is 'stress testing' a business plan?
options: Getting feedback from stressed employees
Working longer hours to complete the plan
Testing it under physical stress
Evaluating how the plan holds up under adverse scenarios to identify where it breaks
exercises.explanation: Stress testing means subjecting your plan to adverse scenarios (recession, supplier failure, regulatory change) to see where it breaks and what needs strengthening.

exercises.question: Why should scenario planning include opportunity mapping, not just risk assessment?
options: Investors prefer optimistic scenarios
Because upside scenarios create opportunities that require advance preparation to capitalize on
To make the plan look more positive
Opportunities are easier to plan for
exercises.explanation: Best-case scenarios create opportunities that require preparation. If you only plan for risks, you might miss the chance to capitalize on unexpected success.

lessons.title: Strategic Thinking with Claude
lessons.description: Use Claude as a strategic advisor for business and career decisions.

sections.content:
Claude as a Strategic Advisor

Claude cannot replace a human advisor who knows your specific situation deeply. But it can serve as an always-available thinking partner that helps you sharpen your strategic thinking.

Framing strategic questions

Good strategic questions for Claude include context: 'I run a 10-person marketing agency that does $2M/year. Our largest client is 40% of revenue. We have been offered a partnership that would require dedicating 3 people full-time. What strategic considerations should I weigh?'

Competitive strategy

Ask Claude to apply established frameworks: 'Apply Porter's Five Forces to analyze my competitive position in the legal tech market. Here is context about my company and competitors: [details].' Claude knows dozens of strategic frameworks and can apply them to your situation.

First principles thinking

'Strip this problem back to first principles. What are the fundamental truths? What assumptions am I making that might be wrong? What would the solution look like if I started from scratch?' This technique is especially powerful when you feel stuck in existing patterns.

Red team / Blue team

Have Claude argue both sides: 'Give me the 3 strongest arguments FOR this acquisition, then the 3 strongest arguments AGAINST. Then give me your honest assessment of which side has the stronger case and why.'

Important caveat

Claude has broad knowledge but lacks the deep, contextual understanding of your specific situation that a human advisor has. Use Claude to explore options, stress test ideas, and sharpen your thinking — but make final strategic decisions with human judgment and real-world context.

exercises.question: What is 'first principles thinking'?
options: Stripping a problem back to its fundamental truths and questioning assumptions
Thinking about things in chronological order
Following the principles of your industry
Always going with your first idea
exercises.explanation: First principles thinking strips away assumptions and conventions to identify the fundamental truths of a problem, then builds solutions from those truths rather than from existing patterns.

exercises.question: What does the 'red team / blue team' technique involve?
options: Splitting your team into two groups
Choosing between two Claude models
Using red and blue text formatting
Having Claude argue both for and against a decision, then assess which side is stronger
exercises.explanation: Red team / blue team has Claude make the strongest case for AND against a decision. This ensures you consider both sides thoroughly before committing.

exercises.question: What is an important limitation to remember when using Claude for strategic advice?
options: Claude always recommends the most expensive option
Claude has broad knowledge but lacks deep, contextual understanding of your specific situation
Strategic thinking requires Claude Opus only
Claude cannot understand business concepts
exercises.explanation: Claude has broad strategic knowledge but lacks the deep, specific context a human advisor develops over time. Use Claude to explore and stress-test ideas, but apply human judgment for final decisions.

courses.title: Advanced Prompting
courses.description: Master advanced prompting techniques including chain-of-thought reasoning, few-shot examples, structured output, and prompt optimization.

lessons.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting
lessons.description: Use chain-of-thought to improve Claude's reasoning on complex problems.

sections.content:
Chain-of-Thought Prompting

Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting is a technique where you ask Claude to show its reasoning step by step before arriving at an answer. This dramatically improves accuracy on complex reasoning tasks.

Why it works

When Claude jumps straight to an answer, it can skip important reasoning steps and arrive at wrong conclusions. Forcing it to think step by step makes it work through the logic explicitly, catching errors along the way. Research shows CoT prompting can improve accuracy by 20-40% on reasoning tasks.

How to trigger chain-of-thought

Simple approaches:

  • 'Think step by step before answering.'
  • 'Show your reasoning process, then give your final answer.'
  • 'Walk me through your analysis before providing a conclusion.'

When to use it

  • Math and logic problems: Any multi-step calculation or logical deduction
  • Complex analysis: When Claude needs to weigh multiple factors
  • Debugging: Working through code logic step by step
  • Legal/medical reasoning: Following chains of rules, exceptions, and conditions

When NOT to use it

Simple factual questions, straightforward writing tasks, and classification tasks do not benefit from chain-of-thought. It adds length without improving quality for simple tasks.

Extended thinking

Claude also supports an 'extended thinking' mode where it reasons extensively before responding. This is automatically enabled on harder tasks and produces even more thorough reasoning than standard CoT prompting.

exercises.question: Why does chain-of-thought prompting improve accuracy?
options: It forces Claude to work through reasoning steps explicitly, catching errors that would occur when jumping straight to an answer
It activates a different Claude model
It makes Claude access a larger database
It costs more tokens, so Claude tries harder
exercises.explanation: Chain-of-thought forces Claude to explicitly work through each reasoning step rather than jumping to a conclusion, making intermediate errors visible and correctable.

exercises.question: Which task would benefit MOST from chain-of-thought prompting?
options: Writing a haiku
A multi-step math word problem requiring logical deduction
Translating a sentence to Spanish
Listing the capital cities of Europe
exercises.explanation: Multi-step math problems with logical deduction require Claude to reason through multiple steps. Chain-of-thought ensures each step is done correctly before proceeding.

exercises.question: What is 'extended thinking' in Claude?
options: A mode where Claude reasons extensively before responding, producing more thorough analysis
Claude taking longer to respond due to server load
A paid add-on feature
A way to make Claude process larger files
exercises.explanation: Extended thinking is a Claude capability where the model reasons extensively before generating its response, going beyond standard chain-of-thought for even more thorough analysis.

lessons.title: Few-Shot Prompting
lessons.description: Teach Claude patterns by providing examples in your prompt.

sections.content:
Few-Shot Prompting

Few-shot prompting means giving Claude examples of the input-output pattern you want before asking it to handle new inputs. It is one of the most powerful prompting techniques because it teaches by showing rather than telling.

Zero-shot vs. few-shot

Zero-shot: 'Classify this email as spam or not spam: [email]' — no examples, just instructions.

Few-shot (2 examples):

Classify emails as spam or not spam.
Email: 'Congratulations! You won $1M. Click here now!' -> spam
Email: 'Meeting rescheduled to 3pm tomorrow. See updated agenda.' -> not spam
Email: 'Limited time offer!!! Buy now and save 90%!!!' ->

Why few-shot works so well

Examples disambiguate your instructions. If you say 'write a short summary,' Claude does not know exactly how short you mean. But if you show three examples of the length and style you want, Claude matches the pattern precisely.

How many examples?

For simple patterns (classification, formatting), 2-3 examples usually suffice. For complex patterns (specific writing style, nuanced judgment), 5-7 examples may be needed. More examples means more context window usage, so find the minimum that works.

Selecting good examples

  • Cover the range of expected inputs (easy cases and edge cases)
  • Show the exact output format you want
  • Include at least one example of each category/outcome
  • Use real examples from your domain, not made-up ones

exercises.question: What is the difference between zero-shot and few-shot prompting?
options: Zero-shot uses Claude Haiku; few-shot uses Opus
Zero-shot gives no examples; few-shot provides examples of the desired input-output pattern
There is no practical difference
Zero-shot is free; few-shot costs money
exercises.explanation: Zero-shot provides only instructions with no examples. Few-shot includes examples of the input-output pattern you want, teaching Claude by showing rather than just telling.

exercises.question: Why are examples more effective than detailed instructions for some tasks?
options: Examples disambiguate exactly what you want (length, style, format) in a way that instructions alone cannot
Examples are required by the API
Claude cannot read instructions
Instructions use more tokens than examples
exercises.explanation: Words like 'brief,' 'formal,' or 'detailed' are subjective. Examples show Claude exactly what you mean, eliminating ambiguity that instructions alone cannot resolve.

exercises.question: What makes a good set of few-shot examples?
options: Only examples of the most common case
As many examples as possible, regardless of quality
Made-up examples that are easy to write
Examples that cover the range of expected inputs including edge cases, with the exact output format you want
exercises.explanation: Good few-shot examples cover the full range of inputs (common and edge cases), demonstrate the exact output format, and include at least one example of each category or outcome.

lessons.title: Structured Output
lessons.description: Get Claude to output JSON, tables, and other structured formats reliably.

sections.content:
Getting Structured Output from Claude

For many tasks, you need Claude's output in a specific structure — JSON, CSV, XML, tables, or other machine-readable formats. Getting reliable structured output requires specific techniques.

JSON output

Be explicit about the schema: 'Output a JSON array of objects with this exact structure: {"name": string, "category": string, "priority": "high" | "medium" | "low", "estimated_hours": number}. No additional text, just the JSON.'

Table output

Specify columns and data types: 'Create a markdown table with columns: Feature, Cost, Implementation Time, Risk Level. Include one row for each recommendation.'

Techniques for reliability

  • Show the schema: Provide an example of the exact structure you want
  • Say 'only output X': 'Only output valid JSON. No explanation, no markdown code fences, just the raw JSON.'
  • Use few-shot examples: Show one complete example of the input and the structured output you expect
  • Validate: For critical applications, parse Claude's output programmatically and handle errors

XML tags for structure

Claude responds well to XML tags for organizing complex outputs:

Respond using this format:
<analysis>Your analysis here</analysis>
<recommendation>Your recommendation</recommendation>
<confidence>high/medium/low</confidence>

XML tags also work well in prompts to delimit sections of input: <context>...</context> <question>...</question>

exercises.question: What is the most reliable way to get JSON output from Claude?
options: Provide the exact schema with field names and types, plus an example of the expected output
Set temperature to 0
Just ask for JSON
Use Claude Haiku, which always outputs JSON
exercises.explanation: Providing the exact JSON schema with field names, types, and an example ensures Claude understands the precise structure you need, producing reliable structured output.

exercises.question: Why are XML tags useful in Claude prompts?
options: They clearly delimit different sections of input and structure the expected output format
Claude only understands XML
XML is faster to process than plain text
They are required for API calls
exercises.explanation: XML tags clearly separate different parts of your prompt (context, question, instructions) and can structure Claude's output format, reducing ambiguity.

exercises.question: How should you handle structured output in critical applications?
options: Only use structured output for non-critical tasks
Trust Claude's output completely
Parse Claude's output programmatically and handle errors
Manually review every character
exercises.explanation: For critical applications, always parse Claude's structured output programmatically and implement error handling for cases where the output does not match the expected schema.

lessons.title: Prompt Optimization
lessons.description: Systematically improve your prompts for better, more consistent results.

sections.content:
Optimizing Your Prompts

Prompt engineering is iterative. Systematic optimization produces prompts that work reliably rather than ones that work sometimes.

The optimization loop

  1. Define success criteria: What does a good response look like? Be specific: format, length, accuracy, tone.
  2. Write your initial prompt
  3. Test with diverse inputs: Try 5-10 different inputs that represent the range of real-world usage
  4. Identify failures: Where did Claude's output not meet your criteria?
  5. Diagnose the cause: Was the instruction ambiguous? Missing an edge case? Wrong format?
  6. Refine the prompt: Fix the specific issue you identified
  7. Re-test: Ensure the fix works without breaking other cases

Common failure patterns and fixes

  • Too verbose: Add explicit length constraints ('under 100 words')
  • Wrong format: Add a few-shot example of the exact format you want
  • Ignoring instructions: Move the instruction to the beginning of the prompt, or repeat it
  • Hallucinating facts: Add 'Only use information from the provided context' or 'Say I don't know if unsure'
  • Inconsistent tone: Add a concrete style example, not just a tone word

A/B testing prompts

For important prompts (production systems, high-volume tasks), test two prompt versions against the same inputs. Score each response against your criteria. Pick the version that performs better overall, not just on one example.

exercises.question: What is the first step in optimizing a prompt?
options: Write the prompt
Choose the right model
Define specific success criteria for what a good response looks like
Set the temperature
exercises.explanation: You cannot optimize without knowing what 'good' looks like. Defining specific success criteria (format, length, accuracy, tone) gives you a clear target to optimize toward.

exercises.question: Claude keeps ignoring an important instruction in your prompt. What should you try first?
options: Move the instruction to the beginning of the prompt or repeat it in a different way
Switch to a different model
Delete the instruction and hope for the best
Use all caps for the instruction
exercises.explanation: Instructions at the beginning of prompts get more attention from Claude. If an instruction is being ignored, moving it to the top or rephrasing and repeating it often resolves the issue.

exercises.question: Why should you test prompts with 5-10 diverse inputs rather than just one?
options: A prompt that works on one input may fail on others — diverse testing reveals edge cases and inconsistencies
Claude needs at least 5 examples to learn
To use up more API credits
It is required by Anthropic's guidelines
exercises.explanation: A prompt might work perfectly on your first test input but fail on others. Testing with diverse inputs reveals edge cases, inconsistencies, and failure modes you would otherwise miss.

lessons.title: Combining Techniques
lessons.description: Combine multiple prompting techniques for maximum effectiveness.

sections.content:
Putting It All Together

The most effective prompts combine multiple techniques. Here is how to layer them for maximum impact.

The full-stack prompt

A production-quality prompt often includes:

  1. Role/persona (system prompt): Sets expertise and communication style
  2. Task description: Clear statement of what you want done
  3. Input context: The data or content to process (delimited with XML tags)
  4. Few-shot examples: 2-3 examples of the desired input-output pattern
  5. Output format: Exact structure of the expected response
  6. Constraints: Length limits, topics to avoid, edge case handling
  7. Chain-of-thought: 'Think step by step' for reasoning tasks

Example: Production classification prompt

System: You are a customer support classifier for an e-commerce company.

User: Classify the following customer message into exactly one category: billing, shipping, product_quality, returns, other. Output JSON with fields: category, confidence (high/medium/low), reasoning (one sentence).

Examples:
Message: 'My package hasn't arrived and it's been 2 weeks' -> {"category": "shipping", "confidence": "high", "reasoning": "Customer reports non-delivery of a package."}
Message: 'The stitching came apart after one wash' -> {"category": "product_quality", "confidence": "high", "reasoning": "Customer reports a manufacturing defect."}

Now classify: <message>I was charged twice for the same order</message>

Knowing when to keep it simple

Not every prompt needs all these layers. A quick question to Claude does not need a system prompt, few-shot examples, and XML tags. Match the prompt complexity to the task complexity. Over-engineering simple prompts wastes time without improving results.

exercises.question: What are the key layers of a production-quality prompt?
options: Just a question and an answer
Role/persona, task description, input context, few-shot examples, output format, constraints, and chain-of-thought as needed
The model name and temperature
Only a system prompt and user message
exercises.explanation: Production-quality prompts layer multiple techniques: persona for expertise, clear task description, delimited input, examples for pattern matching, structured output format, and constraints.

exercises.question: When should you keep a prompt simple rather than using advanced techniques?
options: Never, always use the most complex prompt possible
Only for Claude Haiku
Simple prompts are always better
When the task is straightforward and advanced techniques would add complexity without improving results
exercises.explanation: Match prompt complexity to task complexity. A quick factual question does not need persona, few-shot examples, and XML tags. Over-engineering simple prompts wastes effort.

exercises.question: In the classification prompt example, why is JSON with a 'reasoning' field better than just outputting the category?
options: JSON is required by Claude
The reasoning field provides transparency into Claude's logic, making errors easier to catch and debug
Categories alone are not valid output
It costs fewer tokens
exercises.explanation: Including a reasoning field forces Claude to explain its classification decision, making it easier to identify when Claude misunderstood the input and debug incorrect classifications.
